title: Lapin à la moutarde
subtitle: French rabbit braise in mustard and cream
excerpt: Rabbit joints coated generously in Dijon mustard and browned, then braised in white wine and stock with more mustard stirred in at the end, finished with crème fraîche. A Burgundian classic with a sharp, warming sauce.
sourceNotes: Lapin à la moutarde is a staple of Burgundian home cooking. The Dijon mustard is used twice, as a coating for browning, giving the sauce its backbone, and again stirred in at the end. Elizabeth David describes the dish in 'French Provincial Cooking' (1960). Rabbit is a common farmhouse animal across rural France, and the mustard-cream pairing with rabbit is documented in nineteenth-century French sources.

content.text: Rabbit has a mild flavour that takes mustard well and a lean, close-grained texture that needs moisture to stay tender. The braising method here provides both: the Dijon coating creates a crust when the rabbit is browned, dissolves into the cooking liquid, and concentrates into the sauce when more mustard is added at the end. The result is a sauce that is sharp, complex, and rich without being heavy.

Ask a butcher to joint the rabbit into pieces: two hind legs, two forelegs, and the saddle cut into two. Saddle cooks faster than the legs; the legs need a full 60 minutes and the saddle about 30 to 35 minutes. If you add the saddle pieces 25 minutes into the cook, everything finishes at the same time.

Brush the {{rabbit}} rabbit pieces generously with 2 tablespoons of the Dijon mustard on all sides. Season with salt and pepper. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a wide casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the rabbit pieces in batches without crowding, 3 minutes per side, until golden. Remove and set aside.

Lower the heat to medium. Add the {{shallot}} and {{garlic}} to the casserole and cook for 3 minutes. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine, scraping up the browned bits from the base, and simmer for 2 minutes. Add the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and {{thyme}} of thyme. Return the rabbit legs to the pot (hold the saddle pieces back). Cover and braise over low heat for 25 minutes. Add the saddle pieces and cook for a further 30 minutes. The meat should be coming away from the bone at the joints.

Remove the rabbit. Stir the {{creme-fraiche}} of crème fraîche and the remaining 2 tablespoons of Dijon mustard into the braising liquid. Simmer uncovered for 5 minutes until the sauce thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon. Taste and adjust seasoning. Return the rabbit to the sauce and serve.

items.fix: Add the saddle pieces 25 to 30 minutes into the braise, not at the beginning; ensure the braising liquid covers at least a third of the pieces
Stir in the final mustard off the heat and taste before adding the full amount; crème fraîche softens the sharpness, add more if needed
items.cause: Too much mustard added at the end, or low-quality mustard

items.fix: Brown over medium rather than high heat; the mustard sugars burn at a lower temperature than plain meat
Always stagger the cooking: legs in first for 25 minutes, then saddle for the final 30 minutes
items.cause: All pieces added at the start
items.symptom: Saddle pieces overcooked while legs are not done

content.text: Rabbit with mustard is a Burgundian pairing. The region produces the Dijon mustard and the white wine that go into the sauce, and rabbit has been a farmhouse staple across rural France for centuries. The dish shows up in French home cookery from the nineteenth century on. It is a way of cooking an animal that is lean and quick to dry out. The mustard coats the meat during browning and gives a sharp, warming counterpoint to the cream. In France it is eaten year-round, served with boiled potatoes or egg noodles.
